problem_addressed: The inefficiency of one-class anomaly detection models requiring separate models for each class, increasing cost and memory requirements in Industry 4.0 visual inspection systems.
core_claim: Multi-class unified models perform at par with one-class models for anomaly detection on MVTec AD dataset, eliminating need for separate class-wise models when object classes are significantly different.
key_contributions: Experimental validation that unified multi-class models perform comparably to one-class models on MVTec AD dataset
Deployment and analysis of three lightweight architectures on edge devices (NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX) with quantization techniques
Comparison of quantization-aware training (QAT) and post-training quantization (PTQ) approaches for edge deployment
novelty: Demonstration that separate class-wise models may not be necessary when object classes are significantly different, and exploration of calibration methods for PTQ in unsupervised anomaly detection tasks.
method_summary: The paper implements three knowledge distillation frameworks (US, RD, STFPM) to train unified multi-class models, then applies quantization techniques (PTQ and QAT) for edge deployment, analyzing performance with different precision widths.
method_components: Knowledge distillation frameworks (Uninformed Students, Reverse Distillation, STFPM)
Quantization techniques (Post-Training Quantization with calibration, Quantization-Aware Training)
Feature pyramid matching for enhanced anomaly detection performance
key_innovation: Student-Teacher Feature Pyramid Matching (STFPM) which integrates hierarchical feature matching across multiple scales to enhance anomaly detection at various anomaly sizes.
assumptions: Object classes in the dataset are significantly different from each other
Calibration data can represent the variance encountered during testing
Teacher-student architecture can effectively transfer knowledge across multiple scales
theoretical_grounding: Empirical validation through experiments on MVTec AD dataset; no formal proofs or theoretical bounds provided.
hyperparameters: Teacher/Student architectures: ResNet-18 for STFPM, complex encoder-decoder for RD; Quantization: FP-32, FP-16, INT-8; Calibration: Training data vs Random normal distribution
compute_required: CPU and NVIDIA Jetson NX device
headline_result: STFPM achieves 52.14ms latency with 0.94 AUROC on Jetson (FP-32)
ablations: Quantization precision (FP-32 vs FP-16 vs INT-8)
Calibration strategy (training data vs random normal)
improvement_over_baseline: STFPM is 5x faster than RD on Jetson (52.14ms vs 166.60ms) with only 0.02 AUROC difference; Random normal calibration improves RD AUROC by 0.25 (0.50→0.75) and STFPM by 0.08 (0.75→0.83)
best_result: STFPM achieves 52.14ms latency with 0.94 AUROC on Jetson (FP-32)
title: Unified Anomaly Detection methods on Edge Device using Knowledge Distillation and Quantization
